Software Engineering

DevOps

Programming

Api Design

Clean Code

4 min read
Just now

--

skip_tls: false and the case for treating config as variables, not commands
There’s a line you’ve almost certainly written or read:
skip_tls: falseStop and actually parse it. Don’t skip TLS. Which means… use TLS. You just resolved a double negative to extract a single yes/no fact. That small friction is a design smell, and once you see it you’ll see it everywhere.
The pattern
Configuration keys have a habit of embedding verbs:
disable_cache: false
ignore_errors: true
skip_validation: true
suppress_warnings: true
prevent_deletion: false
force_https: true
exclude_drafts: true
deny_unauthenticated: trueEach of these keys contains an action word — skip, disable, ignore, suppress, prevent, force, exclude, deny, and every verb carries a built-in polarity. “Disable” already points in the negative direction before the boolean says anything. So when you set disable_cache: false, the meaning is split accross two places: half the logic lives in the word disable, half in the value false. You have to compose them to know what’s true.
That’s the core problem. The key is doing semantic work that belongs to the value.
The principle: keys are subjects, values are states
Here’s the mental model that fixes it. A config entry has two halves:

• Left of the colon: the subject the thing being configured.

• Right of the colon: the state. The value that thing holds.
Treat the key like a variable. A variable names a quantity; its value is the quantity. x = 5 doesn’t hide part of the number inside the name x. Config should work the same way: the key names what you’re talking about, and the boolean carries the entire truth.
A verb in the key breaks this. Skip isn’t a subject — it’s a command, and it has direction. Put it on the left and you’ve leaked polarity out of the value and into the name. Now the reader has to reassamble it.
Nouns don’t have direction. cache, validation, tls — these just name a subject and sit still. Only the boolean points anywhere. That’s why the positive, noun-based form reads clean:
cache: true # was: disable_cache: false
validation: false # was: skip_validation: true
verify_tls: false # was: skip_tls: true
warnings_shown: false. # suppress_warnings: true
deletion_allowed: false # prevent_deletion: true
https_only: true # force_https: trueNo Double negatives. No mental composition. You read the subject, you read the state, you’re done.
Rewriting the offenders
Some cases need a little thought, because the right rewrite isn’t always a plain flag:
#Verb-in-key Cleaner-form
disable_cache: false cache: true
skip_validation: true validation: false
ignore_errors: true on_error: "continue"
suppress_warnings: true warnings_shown: false
prevent_deletion: true deletion_allowed: false
force_https: true https_only: true
exclude_drafts: true drafts_visible: false
deny_unauthenticated: true require_auths: trueNote ignore_errors: the honest fix isn’t a boolean at all. “What happens on error” is a mode, not a yes/no, so a string enum (“continue”, “fail”, “retry”) captures the real intent better than any flag. Sometimes hunting the verb reveals that the boolean was the wrong shape to begin with.
The one honest caveat
There’s a legitimate reason authors reach for negative flags, and it’s worth being fair about it. In many languages a missing or unset boolean defaults to false. Consider;

• verify_tls — if omitted, defaults to false -> TLS not verified -> insecure default

• skip_tls — if omitted, defaults to false -> don’t skip -> secure default
The negative flag “fails safe” when the field is absent. That’s read, and it’s why security-sensitive libraries sometimes pick the uglier name.
But notice this is a different axis. That’s about runtime safety — what happens when a values is missing. The verb-in-key problem is about readability — how a present value parses in a human head. You solve the safety concern setting explicit defaults in your loader, not by contorting the key name. Don’t sacrifice clarity to patch a default you could have set directly.
Two things are true at a once: positive noun-keys read better, and you should still control your defaults explicitly so the safe state never depends on a zero value.
The takeaway
Next time naming a config key, run one check:
is there a verb in it?
If there is — skip, disable, ignore, prevent, suppress, force, exclude, deny — you’re probably hiding polarity in the name and forcing your future reader to do arithmetic on a boolean. Pull the verb out. Name the subject as a noun. Let the value hold the truth.
Subject on the left. State on the right. That’s the whole rule.
